 {"id":"bd-1hi.28","title":"§3.5.8-3.5.10 Decode Proofs + Deterministic Encoding + Symbol Size Policy","description":"Implement DecodeProofs, deterministic encoding seed derivation, and symbol size policy (§3.5.8-3.5.10, spec lines 3368-3422).

NOTE: This bead covers THREE distinct spec subsections (§3.5.8, §3.5.9, §3.5.10). Each is relatively small and closely related, so they are tracked together. If any subsection grows in scope during implementation, consider splitting into separate beads.

=== §3.5.8 DECODE PROOFS (Auditable Repair) ===

Asupersync includes a DecodeProof facility (asupersync::raptorq::proof). We exploit this in two critical ways:

1. IN LAB RUNTIME: Every decode that repairs corruption MUST produce a proof artifact attached to the test trace. This makes repair operations auditable and reproducible.

2. IN REPLICATION: A replica MAY demand proof artifacts for suspicious objects (e.g., repeated decode failures), enabling explainable "why did we reject this commit?" answers.

DecodeProof records:
- The set of symbol ESIs received
- Which symbols were repair vs source
- The intermediate decoder state at success/failure
- Timing metadata under LabRuntime (deterministic virtual time)

This is the "alien artifact" stance on repair: we do not merely fix things; we produce a mathematical witness that the fix is correct.

DecodeProof STRUCT:

  DecodeProof := {
      object_id       : ObjectId,
      k_source        : u32,
      symbols_received: Vec<u32>,    // ESIs of all symbols fed to decoder
      source_esis     : Vec<u32>,    // subset that were source symbols
      repair_esis     : Vec<u32>,    // subset that were repair symbols
      decode_success  : bool,
      intermediate_rank: Option<u32>, // decoder matrix rank at success/failure
      timing_ns       : u64,         // wall-clock or virtual time (LabRuntime)
      seed            : u64,         // RaptorQ seed used
  }

=== §3.5.9 DETERMINISTIC ENCODING (Seed Derivation from ObjectId) ===

If ObjectId is content-derived, symbol generation MUST be deterministic:
- The set of source symbols is deterministic by definition (payload chunking).
- Repair symbol generation MUST be deterministic for a given ObjectId and config.

PRACTICAL RULE (normative):
  - Derive any internal "repair schedule seed" from ObjectId:
    seed = xxh3_64(object_id_bytes)
  - Wire it through RaptorQConfig or sender construction as needed.

This makes "the object" a platonic mathematical entity: any replica can regenerate missing repair symbols (within policy) without coordination.

IMPLICATIONS:
1. Two replicas encoding the same object independently produce identical repair symbols.
2. A replica can verify repair symbols received from peers by regenerating them locally.
3. Repair symbols are content-addressed by (ObjectId, ESI) — dedup is automatic.
4. Symbol generation order does not matter; only (ObjectId, config) determines output.

SEED DERIVATION USES (cross-reference):
- ECS objects: seed = xxh3_64(object_id_bytes) (this section)
- Replication changesets: seed = xxh3_64(changeset_id_bytes) (§3.4.2)
- Both use the same pattern: xxh3_64 of the content-addressed identifier bytes.

=== §3.5.10 SYMBOL SIZE POLICY (Object-Type-Aware, Measured) ===

Symbol size is a major performance lever:
- Too small: too many symbols, higher metadata overhead, more routing work.
- Too large: worse cache behavior, higher per-symbol loss impact, more wasted decode work.

POLICY TABLE (verbatim from spec):

| Object Type       | Default Symbol Size      | Rationale |
|-------------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| CommitCapsule     | min(page_size, 4096)     | Aligns encoding with page boundaries; u16-bounded |
| IndexSegment      | 1280-4096 bytes          | Metadata-heavy; smaller symbols reduce tail loss impact |
| CheckpointChunk   | 1024-4096 bytes          | MTU-aware (prefer <=1366 on UDP); large objects use larger K/more blocks rather than huge T |
| PageHistory       | page_size (4096)         | Natural alignment with page boundaries |

NORMATIVE RULES:
1. All sizing is versioned in RootManifest so replicas decode correctly.
2. Benchmarks MUST drive tuning decisions; these defaults are starting points.
3. Symbol size for an object type MUST be consistent across all replicas for a given RootManifest version.
4. Changing symbol size policy requires a RootManifest version bump.

SYMBOL SIZE CONFIGURATION:

  struct SymbolSizePolicy {
      commit_capsule:     fn(page_size: u32) -> u32,  // default: min(page_size, 4096)
      index_segment:      fn(page_size: u32) -> u32,  // default: range 1280-4096
      checkpoint_chunk:   fn(page_size: u32) -> u32,  // default: range 1024-4096
      page_history:       fn(page_size: u32) -> u32,  // default: page_size
  }

  impl Default for SymbolSizePolicy {
      fn default() -> Self {
          Self {
              commit_capsule:   |ps| ps.min(4096),
              index_segment:    |_| 4096,
              checkpoint_chunk: |_| 4096,
              page_history:     |ps| ps,
          }
      }
  }

UNIT TESTS:

§3.5.8 Tests:
1. test_decode_proof_creation:
   Decode an object. DecodeProof MUST contain correct source/repair ESI sets.

2. test_decode_proof_lab_mode:
   Under LabRuntime, timing_ns uses deterministic virtual time.

3. test_decode_proof_failure_case:
   Attempt decode with insufficient symbols. DecodeProof records failure with intermediate_rank.

4. test_decode_proof_auditable:
   Given DecodeProof, verify the decode can be reproduced (same seed, same ESIs -> same result).

5. test_decode_proof_attached_to_trace:
   In lab runtime, every repair decode produces proof attached to test trace.

§3.5.9 Tests:
6. test_seed_derivation_deterministic:
   seed = xxh3_64(object_id_bytes). Same ObjectId -> same seed across calls.

7. test_seed_derivation_different_objects:
   Different ObjectIds -> different seeds (with overwhelming probability).

8. test_deterministic_repair_generation:
   Same ObjectId + same config -> identical repair symbols. Different ObjectId -> different symbols.

9. test_cross_replica_determinism:
   Two independent encoders with same ObjectId produce byte-identical repair symbols.

§3.5.10 Tests:
10. test_symbol_size_commit_capsule:
    page_size=4096: T = min(4096, 4096) = 4096.
    page_size=65536: T = min(65536, 4096) = 4096.

11. test_symbol_size_page_history:
    page_size=4096: T = 4096.
    page_size=65536: T = 65536.

12. test_symbol_size_versioned_in_manifest:
    RootManifest contains symbol size policy version. Replicas use same version.

13. test_symbol_size_consistency:
    All SymbolRecords for an object MUST have same T (OTI.T == symbol_size).

PROPERTY TESTS:

14. prop_seed_no_collision:
    For 100,000 random ObjectIds, all seeds are distinct (probabilistic).

15. prop_symbol_size_within_bounds:
    For all object types and valid page_sizes (512..65536), symbol size is within expected range.

E2E TESTS:

16. test_e2e_decode_proof_on_repair:
    Store object with repair symbols. Corrupt 2 source symbols. Decode succeeds. DecodeProof emitted with correct metadata.

17. test_e2e_deterministic_cross_node:
    Two nodes independently encode same object. Symbols are byte-identical.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- DecodeProof type implemented with all required fields
- Lab runtime always produces proofs on repair decodes
- Seed derivation via xxh3_64(object_id_bytes) is deterministic and wired through
- Symbol size policy per object type implemented with defaults
- Policy versioned in RootManifest
- Cross-replica determinism verified

 {"id":"bd-1hi.29","title":"§3.5.11 Tiered Storage (Bottomless, Native Mode)","description":"Implement tiered storage for Native mode — effectively bottomless history via remote object offload (§3.5.11, spec lines 3424-3498).

THREE TIERS:
- L1 (hot): In-memory caches (ARC for decoded objects + hot pages)
- L2 (warm): Local append-only symbol logs (ecs/symbols/ and ecs/markers/) — default source of truth
- L3 (cold): Remote object storage (S3/R2/Blob) keyed by ObjectId (optionally by (ObjectId, ESI) for symbol-addressable fetch)

REMOTE DURABILITY MODES:
- PRAGMA durability = local: L2 sufficient, L3 optional (archival/time-travel)
- PRAGMA durability = quorum(M): L3/peers participate in durability contract, commit not successful until quorum acknowledges enough symbols

REMOTE TIER INTEGRATION (asupersync normative):
- L3 fetch/upload MUST require RemoteCap in Cx. Without it, fail with explicit error, no network I/O
- Remote operations as named computations (ComputationName, no closure shipping)
- Remote fetch/upload MUST be idempotent (IdempotencyKey derived from request bytes + ecs_epoch)
- Multi-step workflows use Saga discipline: complete or compensations leave system in 'never happened' state

EVICTION POLICY:
- Operates at granularity of rotated log segments (not individual objects)
- MAY evict from L2 only if: every reachable object retrievable from L3 with enough symbols for decode AND segment not needed for in-flight read/repair
- MUST be cancel-safe: keep locally OR prove fully retrievable remotely before deleting

FETCH-ON-DEMAND READ PATH:
1. Try local systematic fast path (§3.5.2)
2. Request missing symbols from L3/peers under Cx budget (source symbols first, then repairs)
3. Decode (emit DecodeProof in lab/debug)
4. Populate L1, optionally write-back repaired symbols to L2 (self-healing cache fill)

RETENTION: Orthogonal to GC horizons. GC horizons = correctness for current ops. Retention = how much history kept for time travel/audit. Default: retain full commit history, cold eligible for L3-only.

PARENT: §3 RaptorQ Foundation (bd-1hi)

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS
- test_l3_fetch_requires_remote_cap: Attempt L3 fetch without RemoteCap in Cx; verify it fails with explicit error and no network I/O occurs
- test_l3_upload_idempotency_key: Upload same object twice with same IdempotencyKey; verify deduplication (second upload returns recorded outcome, not double-write)
- test_eviction_cancel_safety: Start segment eviction (upload to L3), cancel mid-upload; verify local segment is retained and not deleted
- test_eviction_precondition_check: Attempt to evict L2 segment when L3 does not have enough symbols for decode; verify eviction is rejected
- test_fetch_on_demand_systematic_fast_path: Store object with systematic symbols in L2; verify fetch uses fast path (no RaptorQ decode needed for systematic ESIs)
- test_fetch_on_demand_repair_fallback: Store object with missing systematic symbols; verify fetch requests repair symbols from L3 and successfully decodes
- test_durability_mode_local_no_remote: Set PRAGMA durability=local; verify commits succeed without any L3 interaction
- test_durability_mode_quorum_requires_ack: Set PRAGMA durability=quorum(2); verify commit blocks until quorum of peers acknowledge sufficient symbols

## E2E TEST
test_e2e_tiered_storage_evict_and_fetch.rs: Write 500 commits to database, evict cold segments from L2 to a mock L3 store, then read historical data (time-travel query) that requires fetching from L3; verify all pages materialize correctly via fetch-on-demand path with self-healing cache fill back to L2.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] L3 operations are impossible without RemoteCap in Cx (compile-time or runtime enforcement)
- [ ] Eviction is cancel-safe: interrupted eviction never deletes local data before proving remote retrievability
- [ ] Fetch-on-demand correctly falls back from systematic fast path to RaptorQ decode when symbols are missing
- [ ] PRAGMA durability=local commits succeed with zero remote I/O
- [ ] Multi-step eviction workflows follow Saga discipline (complete or compensate to clean state)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:28:46.045459194Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T08:27:54.518165828Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.29","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:28:46.045459194Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.29","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.24","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:44.340388Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.29","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.25","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:44.435316678Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":231,"issue_id":"bd-1hi.29","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Testing Requirements for §3.5-3.6 ECS Substrate + Native Indexes

 {"id":"bd-1hi.3","title":"Understand and Verify RaptorQ Encoding Pipeline (§3.2.3)","description":"Ensure deep understanding and verification of the 5-step RaptorQ encoding process. While FrankenSQLite uses asupersync implementation (not re-implementing RFC 6330), correct integration requires understanding every step.

SPEC REFERENCE: §3.2.3 (Encoding Step by Step)

THE 5 ENCODING STEPS:

STEP 1: DETERMINE CODING PARAMETERS
Given K source symbols C_prime[0], C_prime[1], ..., C_prime[K-1]:

1. Look up K_prime in the systematic index table (RFC 6330 Table 2). K_prime is the smallest value in the table >= K. Examples:
   - K = 5 -> K_prime = 6
   - K = 10 -> K_prime = 10
   - K = 100 -> K_prime = 101

2. Pad source block with (K_prime - K) zero symbols to get exactly K_prime source symbols: C_prime[0], ..., C_prime[K-1], 0, 0, ..., 0.

3. For K_prime, the systematic index table also defines:
   - J(K_prime): the systematic index (used in the Tuple generator)
   - S(K_prime): the number of LDPC symbols
   - H(K_prime): the number of HDPC symbols
   - W(K_prime): the LT generator modulus parameter

4. L = K_prime + S + H: the total number of intermediate symbols.

FrankenSQLite relies on asupersync RFC 6330 implementation for these derivations; do NOT substitute ad-hoc formulas.

STEP 2: CONSTRUCT THE CONSTRAINT MATRIX A (L x L)
The constraint matrix A encodes the relationship between intermediate symbols C[0], ..., C[L-1] and the source/constraint data. A is divided into three regions:

```
A (L x L matrix):
    Rows 0 to S-1:          LDPC constraints (sparse, over GF(2))
    Rows S to S+H-1:        HDPC constraints (dense, over GF(256))
    Rows S+H to L-1:        LT constraints for source symbols (sparse, over GF(2))

         |<--- K_prime cols --->|<- S cols ->|<- H cols ->|
    LDPC |   LDPC_LEFT         | I_S(SxS)   |   0        |  S rows
    HDPC |   MT * GAMMA        |   0         | I_H(HxH)  |  H rows
    LT   |   LT_MATRIX         |   0         |   0        |  K_prime rows
```

LDPC ROWS (0..S-1):
Each LDPC row has approximately 3 * ceil(K_prime/S) non-zero entries in the leftmost K_prime columns, plus a 1 on the diagonal of the S x S identity block. Sparse and binary (GF(2)).

LDPC constraint generation (RFC 6330 §5.3.3.3):
```
For j = 0 to K_prime-1:
    a = 1 + floor(j / S)
    b = j % S
    A[b][j] = 1
    b = (b + a) % S
    A[b][j] = 1
    b = (b + a) % S
    A[b][j] = 1
```
Additionally, column K_prime + i is set to 1 for row i (the S x S identity block).
Each source column contributes exactly 3 nonzeros, total LDPC nonzeros = 3*K_prime.

HDPC ROWS (S..S+H-1):
Dense over GF(256). Generated using:
1. The MT matrix (H x (K_prime+S)), computed from a random walk using the Rand function
2. The GAMMA matrix ((K_prime+S) x (K_prime+S)), structured matrix over GF(256) defined by alpha (primitive element)
These rows provide the algebraic strength that makes RaptorQ achieve near-optimal failure probability. They are the reason GF(256) is used.

LT ROWS (S+H..L-1):
Row S+H+i corresponds to source symbol C_prime[i]. Generated by the Tuple function:
```
(d, a, b, d1, a1, b1) = Tuple(K_prime, i)

Row S+H+i has 1s at positions:
    b                          (always)
    (b + a) mod W              (if d >= 2)
    (b + 2*a) mod W            (if d >= 3)
    ...
    (b + (d-1)*a) mod W        (if degree is d)
Plus "permanent inactivation" entries from d1, a1, b1 in columns W..K_prime-1
```

STEP 3: BUILD THE SOURCE VECTOR D (L entries)
```
D[0..S-1]      = zero symbols (LDPC constraints have zero right-hand side)
D[S..S+H-1]    = zero symbols (HDPC constraints have zero right-hand side)
D[S+H..L-1]    = C_prime[0], C_prime[1], ..., C_prime[K_prime-1] (padded source symbols)
```

STEP 4: SOLVE A * C = D FOR INTERMEDIATE SYMBOLS
Find intermediate symbols C[0], ..., C[L-1] such that A * C = D.
- A is L x L and invertible (by construction for valid K_prime)
- Standard linear system solve over GF(256)
- Gaussian elimination with nonzero pivot selection
- Matrix A structure (sparse LDPC + dense HDPC + sparse LT) is amenable to efficient elimination via inactivation decoding (§3.2.4)

STEP 5: GENERATE ENCODING SYMBOLS
Given intermediate symbols, any encoding symbol with Internal Symbol ID (ISI) X can be generated:

```
generate_symbol(X, K_prime, C[0..L-1]):
    if X < K_prime:
        return C_prime[X]    // systematic: return source symbol itself
    else:
        return LTEnc(K_prime, C[0..L-1], X)
```

LTEnc function for ISI X >= K_prime:
```
LTEnc(K_prime, C[0..L-1], X):
    (d, a, b, d1, a1, b1) = Tuple(K_prime, X)
    result = C[b]
    for j in 1..d:
        b = (b + a) mod W
        result = result XOR C[b]
    // Permanent inactivation component
    while b1 >= L:
        b1 = (b1 + a1) mod P1
    result = result XOR C[b1]
    for j in 1..d1:
        b1 = (b1 + a1) mod P1
        while b1 >= L:
            b1 = (b1 + a1) mod P1
        result = result XOR C[b1]
    return result
```

KEY INSIGHT -- SYSTEMATIC PROPERTY:
For ISI X < K_prime, the encoding symbol is EXACTLY the source symbol C_prime[X]. This means in the no-loss case, the receiver already has all K source symbols and no decoding is needed. Repair symbols (ISI >= K_prime) are generated ONLY as redundancy.

VERIFICATION TEST CASES:
1. For K=5: encode, verify first 5 encoding symbols are identity (systematic property)
2. For K=100: encode, verify first 100 symbols are identity, generate 10 repair symbols
3. Constraint matrix A: verify dimensions are L x L, verify LDPC rows are sparse/binary, verify HDPC rows are dense/GF(256), verify LT rows match Tuple output
4. Intermediate symbol solve: verify A * C = D after solving
5. Round-trip: encode K symbols, generate K+2 repair symbols, verify decode recovers original
6. Zero-padding: K=5, K_prime=6, verify the 6th source symbol is zero
7. LTEnc: spot-check specific ISIs against known-good asupersync output

E2E TEST:
- Encode database pages (K=64, page_size=4096), generate 4 repair symbols (ISIs 64..67)
- Verify source symbols 0..63 are identity (page data unchanged)
- Drop 2 random source symbols, replace with repair symbols, decode, verify all 64 pages recovered
- Encode with K=56403 (max), verify no panics/overflows

CRATE: Integration tests in fsqlite-harness against asupersync::raptorq
ACCEPTANCE: End-to-end encoding test passes. Systematic property verified. Repair symbols decode correctly. Constraint matrix structure verified.

-{"id":"bd-1hi.30","title":"§3.5.12 Adaptive Redundancy (Anytime-Valid Durability Autopilot)","description":"Unit Test Requirements:
- test_bd_1hi_30_unit_compliance_gate: concrete unit identifier for structure lint compliance.
- prop_bd_1hi_30_structure_compliance: concrete property identifier for structure lint compliance.

Implement adaptive redundancy control loop with formal guarantees (§3.5.12, spec lines 3499-3627).

CORE THESIS: Static redundancy assumptions are a correctness risk. RaptorQ redundancy is a control loop with formal guarantees: monitor symbol health with anytime-valid tests, raise redundancy when evidence indicates durability budget violated.

KEY ENABLING FACT: Repair symbol generation is deterministic → redundancy is appendable (add repair symbols later without changing ObjectId or rewriting).

DURABILITY BUDGETS (§3.5.12.1, per object type):
- p_symbol_budget: max acceptable symbol corruption probability per record
- epsilon_loss_budget: max acceptable probability object becomes undecodable
- slack_symbols: additive decode slack per source block (V1 default +2)
- CommitMarker/CommitProof MUST use conservative budgets (small objects clamp)

E-PROCESS MONITORING (§3.5.12.2):
- Bernoulli observation each symbol verify: X=1 if corrupt, X=0 otherwise
- Monitor H0: p <= p0 (p0 = p_symbol_budget). E-value > 1/alpha → reject (Ville's inequality)
- Monitoring MUST be separated from hot path: batch observations in decode/verification bookkeeping

LIVING CORRUPTION-RATE ESTIMATES (§3.5.12.2.1):
- Bayesian posterior: Beta(alpha0 + n_bad, beta0 + n_ok). Surface posterior mean and 99.9% credible bound
- IMPORTANT: Bayesian bounds NOT anytime-valid under optional stopping — diagnostics ONLY
- Safety-critical decisions MUST use anytime-valid bound p_upper (e-process inversion/martingale)
- PolicyController MAY use Bayesian for expected-loss ranking, MUST treat e-process as hard guardrail

AUTOPILOT POLICY (§3.5.12.3, when INV-SYMBOL-CORRUPTION rejects):
1. Raise redundancy for new objects: overhead := min(overhead_max, max(overhead_min, overhead * 2))
2. Retroactive hardening (background): generate+persist additional repair symbols for reachable objects. Union-only: can't invalidate prior decodes
3. Escalate integrity sweeps: increase frequency+sampling
4. Emit explainable evidence: evidence ledger with rejection, policy change, hardened objects

GRACEFUL DEGRADATION: If retroactive hardening can't decode (insufficient survivors) → surface 'durability contract violated' with decode proofs, halt operations claiming durable commit for unverifiable objects.

ALIEN-ARTIFACT QUALITY: Formal safety guarantees (optional stopping safe), explainability (evidence ledgers), self-healing (append-only deterministic), graceful degradation (repair or prove).

PARENT: §3 RaptorQ Foundation (bd-1hi)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Adaptive redundancy adjusts RaptorQ symbol overhead based on observed channel loss rate
- [ ] Anytime-valid durability guarantees hold under optional stopping (no matter when observation ceases)
- [ ] Evidence ledger entries for redundancy decisions are append-only and deterministically reproducible
- [ ] Self-healing repairs are triggered automatically when symbol loss exceeds configured threshold
- [ ] Graceful degradation: when repair is impossible, system produces a proof of insufficiency rather than silent corruption","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:28:49.005538557Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T03:13:33.481242748Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:28:49.005538557Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.22","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:44.530711840Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:44.626115387Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":104,"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §3.5.12.4 Why This Is Alien-Artifact Quality (Spec Extract)

This sub-subsection existed in the spec but was not explicitly captured in beads with the `§3.5.12.4` anchor.

+{"id":"bd-1hi.30","title":"§3.5.12 Adaptive Redundancy (Anytime-Valid Durability Autopilot)","description":"Unit Test Requirements:

- prop_bd_1hi_30_structure_compliance: concrete property identifier for structure lint compliance.

Implement adaptive redundancy control loop with formal guarantees (§3.5.12, spec lines 3499-3627).

CORE THESIS: Static redundancy assumptions are a correctness risk. RaptorQ redundancy is a control loop with formal guarantees: monitor symbol health with anytime-valid tests, raise redundancy when evidence indicates durability budget violated.

KEY ENABLING FACT: Repair symbol generation is deterministic → redundancy is appendable (add repair symbols later without changing ObjectId or rewriting).

DURABILITY BUDGETS (§3.5.12.1, per object type):
- p_symbol_budget: max acceptable symbol corruption probability per record
- epsilon_loss_budget: max acceptable probability object becomes undecodable
- slack_symbols: additive decode slack per source block (V1 default +2)
- CommitMarker/CommitProof MUST use conservative budgets (small objects clamp)

E-PROCESS MONITORING (§3.5.12.2):
- Bernoulli observation each symbol verify: X=1 if corrupt, X=0 otherwise
- Monitor H0: p <= p0 (p0 = p_symbol_budget). E-value > 1/alpha → reject (Ville's inequality)
- Monitoring MUST be separated from hot path: batch observations in decode/verification bookkeeping

LIVING CORRUPTION-RATE ESTIMATES (§3.5.12.2.1):
- Bayesian posterior: Beta(alpha0 + n_bad, beta0 + n_ok). Surface posterior mean and 99.9% credible bound
- IMPORTANT: Bayesian bounds NOT anytime-valid under optional stopping — diagnostics ONLY
- Safety-critical decisions MUST use anytime-valid bound p_upper (e-process inversion/martingale)
- PolicyController MAY use Bayesian for expected-loss ranking, MUST treat e-process as hard guardrail

AUTOPILOT POLICY (§3.5.12.3, when INV-SYMBOL-CORRUPTION rejects):
1. Raise redundancy for new objects: overhead := min(overhead_max, max(overhead_min, overhead * 2))
2. Retroactive hardening (background): generate+persist additional repair symbols for reachable objects. Union-only: can't invalidate prior decodes
3. Escalate integrity sweeps: increase frequency+sampling
4. Emit explainable evidence: evidence ledger with rejection, policy change, hardened objects

GRACEFUL DEGRADATION: If retroactive hardening can't decode (insufficient survivors) → surface 'durability contract violated' with decode proofs, halt operations claiming durable commit for unverifiable objects.

ALIEN-ARTIFACT QUALITY: Formal safety guarantees (optional stopping safe), explainability (evidence ledgers), self-healing (append-only deterministic), graceful degradation (repair or prove).

PARENT: §3 RaptorQ Foundation (bd-1hi)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Adaptive redundancy adjusts RaptorQ symbol overhead based on observed channel loss rate
- [ ] Anytime-valid durability guarantees hold under optional stopping (no matter when observation ceases)
- [ ] Evidence ledger entries for redundancy decisions are append-only and deterministically reproducible
- [ ] Self-healing repairs are triggered automatically when symbol loss exceeds configured threshold
- [ ] Graceful degradation: when repair is impossible, system produces a proof of insufficiency rather than silent corruption","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:28:49.005538557Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T10:26:56.663088826Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T10:26:56.663066595Z","close_reason":"Implemented adaptive redundancy autopilot with p_upper hard guardrail, evidence-context retunes, and bd-1hi.30 unit/property/e2e compliance tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:28:49.005538557Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.22","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:44.530711840Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:44.626115387Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":104,"issue_id":"bd-1hi.30","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §3.5.12.4 Why This Is Alien-Artifact Quality (Spec Extract)

 {"id":"bd-1hi.31","title":"§3.6.1-3.6.3 Native Index Types: VersionPointer + IndexSegments","description":"Implement VersionPointer and IndexSegment types for Native Mode coded indexing (§3.6.1-3.6.3, spec lines 3628-3658).

THE INDEX MUST ANSWER: Given (pgno, snapshot), find newest committed version V where V.commit_seq <= snapshot.high, plus pointer to bytes/intent recipe to materialize V.

VERSION POINTER (atom of lookup):
  VersionPointer { commit_seq: u64, patch_object: ObjectId, patch_kind: PatchKind (FullImage|IntentLog|SparseXor), base_hint: Option<ObjectId> }
Stable and replicable: references content-addressed objects, not physical offsets.

INDEX SEGMENT TYPES (all ECS objects):
1. PageVersionIndexSegment: Maps Pgno → VersionPointer for specific commit range. Includes bloom filters for fast 'not present' checks
2. ObjectLocatorSegment: Maps ObjectId → Vec<SymbolLogOffset>. Accelerator for finding symbols on disk. Rebuildable by scanning symbol logs
3. ManifestSegment: Maps commit_seq ranges to IndexSegment ObjectIds. Used for bootstrapping

PARENT: §3 RaptorQ Foundation (bd-1hi)

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS
- test_version_pointer_serialization_roundtrip: Serialize and deserialize VersionPointer with all PatchKind variants (FullImage, IntentLog, SparseXor) and with/without base_hint; verify field-level equality
- test_page_version_index_segment_lookup: Insert 50 (pgno, VersionPointer) entries covering commit range [10,60], verify lookup returns correct newest version for a given (pgno, snapshot.high)
- test_page_version_index_segment_bloom_filter: Verify bloom filter returns false for pages not in segment (zero false negatives) and measure false positive rate stays below configured threshold
- test_object_locator_segment_rebuild: Build ObjectLocatorSegment, then rebuild from scanning symbol logs; verify rebuilt segment produces identical lookups
- test_manifest_segment_bootstrap: Create ManifestSegment mapping commit_seq ranges to IndexSegment ObjectIds, verify bootstrap can locate correct segment for any commit_seq
- test_version_pointer_references_content_addressed: Verify VersionPointer.patch_object is a valid ObjectId (BLAKE3 content hash), not a physical offset
- test_index_segment_is_ecs_object: Verify each segment type can be encoded as an ECS object with valid ObjectId, symbol records, and repair symbols

## E2E TEST
test_e2e_native_index_lookup_path.rs: Commit 200 transactions modifying overlapping pages, build PageVersionIndexSegments covering the full commit range, then read each page under 10 different snapshot values and verify the lookup algorithm (§3.6.4) returns the correct newest visible version for each snapshot.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] VersionPointer correctly encodes all three PatchKind variants and round-trips through serialization
- [ ] PageVersionIndexSegment bloom filter has zero false negatives for contained pages
- [ ] ObjectLocatorSegment is rebuildable from symbol log scan with identical results
- [ ] ManifestSegment correctly maps arbitrary commit_seq to the covering IndexSegment
- [ ] All index segments are valid ECS objects (content-addressed, repairable via RaptorQ)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:07.961182494Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T19:25:28.954746667Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T19:25:28.954724716Z","close_reason":"Implemented PatchKind, VersionPointer, SymbolLogOffset, BloomFilter, PageVersionIndexSegment, ObjectLocatorSegment, ManifestSegment in fsqlite-types/src/ecs.rs. 30 total ECS tests pass (8 new for index types).  {"id":"bd-1hi.32","title":"§3.6.4-3.6.5 Native Index Lookup Algorithm + Segment Construction","description":"Unit Test Requirements:
- test_bd_1hi_32_unit_compliance_gate: concrete unit identifier for structure lint compliance.
- prop_bd_1hi_32_structure_compliance: concrete property identifier for structure lint compliance.

Implement the read-path lookup algorithm and background segment construction for coded indexes (§3.6.4-3.6.5, spec lines 3659-3677).

LOOKUP ALGORITHM (read page P under snapshot S):
1. Check Cache: Consult ARC cache for visible committed version
2. Check Filter: Version Presence Filter (Bloom/Quotient). If 'no versions', read base page
3. Index Scan: Scan PageVersionIndexSegments backwards from S.high until visible version found
4. Fetch and Materialize: Fetch patch_object (repair via RaptorQ if needed). If full image → return. If patch/intent → apply to base page (recursively if needed)

SEGMENT CONSTRUCTION (background, deterministic):
- Segment Builder consumes commit marker stream
- Accumulates Pgno → VersionPointer updates in memory
- Periodically flushes new PageVersionIndexSegment covering [start_seq, end_seq]
- Construction is DETERMINISTIC: stable map iteration order, stable encoding → all replicas build identical index segments

TESTS: lookup with cache hit, lookup with bloom filter negative, lookup through index scan, patch materialization (full image + intent log + sparse XOR), segment builder round-trip, determinism verification (same input → same segment ObjectId).

PARENT: §3 RaptorQ Foundation (bd-1hi)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Native index lookup returns correct results for point queries and range scans across all stored key types
- [ ] Segment construction round-trips correctly: build segment from data, read back, verify identical content
- [ ] Patch materialization correctly combines full image + intent log + sparse XOR to produce final page state

1. **test_object_id_blake3_derivation**: ObjectId = Trunc128(BLAKE3("fsqlite:ecs:v1" || canonical_bytes)). Verify deterministic.
2. **test_object_id_collision_resistance**: Different content → different ObjectIds (BLAKE3 collision resistance).
3. **test_object_id_deterministic**: Same content → same ObjectId (content-addressed).
4. **test_object_id_display_hex**: ObjectId displays as 32-char hex string for debugging.

### §3.5.2 SymbolRecord Envelope (bd-1hi.21)
5. **test_symbol_record_envelope**: SymbolRecord contains: object_id, esi (encoding symbol index), data, auth_tag.
6. **test_symbol_record_auth_tag**: Auth tag = BLAKE3(object_id || esi || data). Verify tamper detection.
7. **test_symbol_record_tampered_rejected**: Modified data with original auth_tag → rejected.
8. **test_symbol_record_serialization**: Round-trip serialize/deserialize SymbolRecord.

### §3.5.3 Deterministic Repair Symbol Generation (bd-1hi.22)
9. **test_repair_deterministic**: Same source data + same K,R → identical repair symbols.
10. **test_repair_independent_of_order**: Repair generation order doesn't affect output symbols.
11. **test_repair_from_any_seed**: Given seed, repair symbols are reproducible.
12. **test_repair_lab_mode_identical**: Under LabRuntime, repair generation is byte-identical across runs.

### §3.5.4 Commit Marker Stream Format (bd-1hi.23)
13. **test_marker_stream_append_only**: Markers are append-only. No in-place modification.
14. **test_marker_format**: CommitMarker contains: commit_seq, capsule_object_id, timestamp, writer_id.
15. **test_marker_is_point_of_no_return**: Once marker is fsync'd, commit is durable.
16. **test_marker_stream_scan_forward**: Recovery scans markers forward from last checkpoint.

### §3.5.4.2 Symbol Record Logs (bd-1hi.24)
17. **test_symbol_log_append_only**: Symbol records appended to log files.
18. **test_symbol_log_indexed**: Symbols indexed by object_id + esi for O(1) lookup.
19. **test_symbol_log_gc_safe**: GC removes symbols below gc_horizon only.

### §3.5.6 Inter-Object Coding for Replication (bd-1hi.26)
20. **test_inter_object_coding**: Multiple ECS objects encoded together for bandwidth efficiency.
21. **test_inter_object_decode**: Receiver decodes inter-object coded stream → individual objects.

### §3.5.7 RaptorQ Permeation Map Audit (bd-1hi.27)
22. **test_permeation_map_complete**: Every subsystem that stores data has RaptorQ protection.
23. **test_permeation_map_no_gaps**: No unprotected data paths exist (audit checklist).

### §3.5.8-3.5.10 Decode Proofs + Deterministic Encoding + Symbol Size (bd-1hi.28)
24. **test_decode_proof_certificate**: After decode, DecodeProof certificate witnesses reconstruction.
25. **test_decode_proof_verifiable**: DecodeProof can be verified independently without re-decoding.
26. **test_deterministic_encoding**: Same source + same parameters → byte-identical encoded output.
27. **test_symbol_size_policy**: Symbol size T respects configured page_size and alignment.

### §3.5.11 Tiered Storage (bd-1hi.29)
28. **test_tiered_storage_local_first**: Symbols stored locally first, replicated to remote tier.
29. **test_tiered_storage_remote_fetch**: On local miss, fetch from remote tier with RemoteCap.
30. **test_tiered_storage_eviction**: Local tier evicts cold symbols to remote tier.

### §3.5.12 Adaptive Redundancy (bd-1hi.30)
31. **test_redundancy_autopilot**: e-process monitors symbol corruption budget. Adjusts R when drift detected.
32. **test_redundancy_increases_on_corruption**: Observed corruption → redundancy increases automatically.
33. **test_redundancy_evidence_logged**: Redundancy changes logged in evidence ledger.

### §3.6.1-3.6.3 Native Index Types (bd-1hi.31)
34. **test_version_pointer_index**: VersionPointer maps (page_number, commit_seq) → ECS object_id.
35. **test_index_segment_construction**: IndexSegments built from committed version data.
36. **test_index_lookup_o1**: Index lookup is O(1) for known (page, commit_seq).

### §3.6.4-3.6.5 Native Index Lookup + Segment Construction (bd-1hi.32)
37. **test_lookup_latest_version**: Lookup returns newest version with commit_seq <= snapshot.high.
38. **test_segment_construction_deterministic**: Same input → same segments (reproducible).

### §3.6.6-3.6.7 Index Repair/Rebuild + Boldness (bd-1hi.33)
39. **test_index_rebuild_from_markers**: Full index rebuild by replaying commit marker stream.
40. **test_index_repair_from_ecs**: Corrupted index entries repaired from ECS symbols.
41. **test_boldness_constraint**: Index operations respect boldness constraint (no speculative reads beyond confirmed).

### E2E Tests
42. **test_e2e_ecs_full_lifecycle**: Create objects → encode → store → corrupt → decode → verify → GC.
43. **test_e2e_tiered_eviction_and_fetch**: Local tier full → evict → remote fetch → data recovered.

### Logging Requirements
- DEBUG: ObjectId computation, symbol record operations, index lookups
- INFO: ECS object lifecycle (create/store/decode/GC), redundancy changes
- WARN: Decode with minimum symbols, tiered storage fetch latency high
- ERROR: Decode failure, auth tag mismatch, permeation gap detected

 {"id":"bd-1hi.33","title":"§3.6.6-3.6.7 Native Index Repair/Rebuild + Boldness Constraint","description":"Unit Test Requirements:
- test_bd_1hi_33_unit_compliance_gate: concrete unit identifier for structure lint compliance.
- prop_bd_1hi_33_structure_compliance: concrete property identifier for structure lint compliance.

Implement index repair, rebuild, and enforce the boldness constraint (§3.6.6-3.6.7, spec lines 3678-3692).

REPAIR AND REBUILD (because IndexSegments are ECS objects):
- Repair: Missing/corrupt segments repaired by decoding from surviving symbols (local or remote)
- Rebuild: If segment irretrievably lost, rebuild by re-scanning commit marker stream and capsules
- Diagnostics: 'Index unrebuildable but commit markers exist' = critical integrity failure

BOLDNESS CONSTRAINT: Coded index segments ship in V1. NOT a 'Phase 9 nice-to-have'. The index is part of the fundamental ECS thesis: if durability, storage, and transport are all object-based and symbol-native, then the index MUST be too. Fallbacks (linear marker-stream scan) exist ONLY as emergency escape hatches, activated only after conformance/performance data proves a need.

TESTS: repair from surviving symbols, full rebuild from marker stream scan, critical integrity failure detection, fallback linear scan (emergency only).

PARENT: §3 RaptorQ Foundation (bd-1hi)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Native index repair reconstructs a valid index from surviving RaptorQ symbols without full rebuild
- [ ] Full rebuild from marker stream scan produces an index identical to the original (deterministic)
- [ ] Critical integrity failures are detected and reported with specific error codes before any repair attempt
- [ ] Fallback linear scan (emergency mode) correctly locates all entries when index is completely destroyed
- [ ] Boldness constraint limits repair aggressiveness to prevent cascading corruption","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:26.826958932Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T09:56:12.024485629Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T09:56:12.024463026Z","close_reason":"Implemented §3.6.6-§3.6.7 native index repair/rebuild + boldness with required tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.33","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:26.826958932Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.33","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.22","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:46.394699666Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.33","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.24","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:46.489396080Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1hi.33","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.32","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:29:46.301421706Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":235,"issue_id":"bd-1hi.33","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Testing Requirements for §3.5-3.6 ECS Substrate + Native Indexes
